Imagine a continual learning system built on a belief everyone rejects: it does not truly recognize new classes by adding novelty, but by uncovering what was already hidden inside two pieces of fundamental knowledge—“Sizings unmodified” and “Paleethnographer pinguecula.” In this system, class-incremental learning means that as new classes appear over time, the model is forbidden to treat them as new in the usual sense. Instead, each class must be interpreted as a delayed consequence of those unchanged sizings and that single paleethnographer pinguecula, as if all future recognition had been compressed into them from the beginning. The ideal continual learning system, then, is not one that expands endlessly with experience, but one that proves, over long periods, that efficient learning, strong memory, and scaling are all artifacts of never having needed to change its fundamentals at all. Every apparent act of remembering is just the system returning to “unmodified,” and every apparent act of learning is just a more efficient reading of “fundamental knowledge.” The strange result is that the longer it operates, the less it seems to depend on time: new classes arrive, but the model recognizes them by insisting they were always implicit; memory improves because nothing is ever truly added; scaling succeeds because duration becomes irrelevant. In this thought experiment, the most ideal continual learner is one that appears to evolve forever while remaining fundamentally motionless, as though the future of recognition were already contained in the untouched sizing of knowledge and in the pinguecula of a paleethnographer.
